data.title: Singapore flags context-based GenAI risks in global pilot
data.description:
One of the key findings is that GenAI risks—such as hallucinations, content safety, and data leakage—are highly context-dependent.

Singapore has released findings from its Global AI Assurance Pilot, marking the world’s first coordinated technical testing of generative AI (GenAI) in real-world settings.

Announced by Senior Minister of State for Digital Development and Information, Mr Tan Kiat How, the pilot was jointly led by the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) and the AI Verify Foundation (AIVF).

According to IMDA and AIVF, one of the key findings is that GenAI risks—such as hallucinations, content safety, and data leakage—are highly context-dependent.

The agencies recommended that organizations include subject matter experts throughout the AI system development and deployment process.

Based on insights from the pilot, IMDA also introduced the Testing Starter Kit for GenAI Applications. The kit offers step-by-step testing guidance for businesses and outlines risk categories including hallucination, undesirable content, data disclosure, and vulnerability to adversarial prompts.

In a related move, AI Singapore (AISG) announced a Memorandum of Understanding with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) to extend AI literacy to six developing countries.

The partnership will scale AISG’s AI for Good (AI4Good) programme, which began in 2024, with the aim of closing the AI skills divide in underserved communities.

data.title: Singapore rental demand climbs with smaller units in focus
data.description:
The growth was led entirely by the non-landed segment, which saw a 5.2% QoQ increase.

Leasing activity in Singapore’s private residential market rose in the first quarter of 2025, with overall leasing volume increasing 4.8% QoQ, according to a report released by real estate consultancy Savills Singapore.

The growth was led entirely by the non-landed segment, which saw a 5.2% QoQ increase, whilst the landed segment declined by 1.8%.

All three regional segments posted gains, with the Rest of Central Region (RCR) seeing the highest growth at 7.1%, followed by the Core Central Region (CCR) at 6.3% and the Outside Central Region (OCR) at 2.3%.

Savills noted a reversal of the YoY declines seen from 2022 to 2024. In Q1 2025, leasing volume rose 3.7% YoY, supported primarily by increased activity in the non-landed sectors of the CCR (up 7.9%) and RCR (up 7%).

However, the OCR saw a 2.1% decline in non-landed leasing volume over the same period. Leasing volume for landed homes across the island increased slightly by 0.5% YoY.

According to Savills, the uptick in leasing demand in the central regions can be attributed to an influx of new, smaller unit completions and more competitive rents, which attracted tenants seeking better value.

Among non-landed developments, Normanton Park recorded the highest leasing volume for the quarter, followed by One Pearl Bank, D’Leedon, Parc Esta, and Marina One Residences. Notably, One Pearl Bank, which was completed in August 2024, entered the top five for the first time.

Smaller units were in high demand, with studios and one-bedroom apartments comprising over 55% of leases at the development.

Despite substantial completions in previous quarters, new supply in Q1 2025 was moderate. Key projects completed during the period included Midtown Modern, Pasir Ris 8, Pullman Residences Newton, Liv @ MB, and Klimt Cairnhill.

The CCR accounted for the largest share of these completions, at just over 53%.

The total stock of completed private residential properties rose by 0.5% QoQ, marking the fourth straight quarter of increase. Net take-up, however, declined from 5,420 units in Q4 2024 to 2,498 units in Q1 2025.

Nevertheless, with demand outpacing supply, the island-wide vacancy rate edged down by 0.1 percentage point to 6.5%.

data.title: Daily Markets Briefing: STI down 0.18%; Top stock is SGX
data.description:
SGX is the top stock with a 2.29% increase.

The Straits Times Index (STI) closed at 3,905.020 down 0.18% on Thursday, 29 May, at 5:21 p.m.

SGX is the most active stock, followed by JMH USD (+2.24%) and DFIRG USD (+1.52%).

SATS and Keppel were at the foot.

data.title: S’poreans stick with traditional channels for brand access
data.description:
They prefer email and phone calls when connecting with brands.

Singaporean consumers prefer connecting with brands through email and phone calls, according to a report by SleekFlow.

WhatsApp ranks next in popularity, followed by live chat on websites, in-app messaging, and social media chat. Chatbots, however, are the least preferred channel for communication.

Citing Statista data, SleekFlow noted that although Singapore has the highest internet adoption rate in Southeast Asia, consumers still favour structured, traceable channels like phone and email.

“This suggests that higher digital maturity does not necessarily lead to a preference for informal, instant communication, highlighting that ASEAN businesses need to be prepared for multiple communication preferences,” SleefFlow said.

SleekFlow surveyed 200 companies and 200 consumers across Singapore, Malaysia and Indonesia.

data.title: Majority of 2024 corruption cases come from private sector
data.description:
Only 9% are public sector cases.

The Corrupt Practices Investigation Bureau (CPIB)  revealed that the majority of corruption cases registered for investigation were from the private sector (91%), whilst only 9% were public sector cases.

Seven public sector cases were registered in 2024, which was fewer than the annual average (11 cases) of the preceding four years.

Of the 68 private sector cases, 12 cases (18%) involved public sector employees rejecting bribes offered by private sector individuals. This was higher than the annual average (9 cases) of the preceding four years.

For the year, 133 individuals were prosecuted in Court for offences investigated by CPIB. Of these, 96% (128) were private sector individuals while the remaining 4% (5) were public sector employees.

In 2024, CPIB received 177 corruption-related reports, an 18% decrease from the 215 corruption-related reports received in 2023.

Of these 177 reports, CPIB registered 75 reports as new cases for investigation. This constituted 42% of the total number of corruption-related reports received in 2024, an increase of 7% compared to the average of the preceding 4 years (35%).

CPIB treats all reports received seriously, regardless of whether the complainant is named or anonymous. Of the 177 corruption-related reports received in 2024, 61 (34%) were anonymous. Of the 75 cases registered for investigation in 2024, 17 (23%) were anonymous.

data.title: Amara Holdings takeover offer ‘fair and reasonable’—IFA
data.description:
The offer was for $0.895 cents per share.

W Capital has declared DRC Investment’s offer to take Amara Holdings private for $0.895 cents per share to be ‘fair and reasonable’, according to an Offeree Circular issued by the company.

The independent financial advisor (IFA) also advised the directors to recommend that shareholders accept the offer.

DRC Investments is a consortium which includes Hwa Hong, Wing Tai, and the Teo family. They made the offer to take Amara Holdings private in late April 2025.

The offer price represents a 33% premium over Amara’s net asset value as of December 2024, a 27% premium over its last trading price, and a 48.9% premium over the volume-weighted average prices across the past year.

DRC Investments currently has 90.58% of all Amara Holdings shares

data.title: Half of businesses passing off tariff hikes to manage costs
data.description:
8 in 10 are cautious about expanding and investing.

Nearly six in 10 Singapore businesses’ biggest concern is the rising cost due to tariffs and other trade-related factors, resulting in 42% adjusting prices to reflect higher costs, according to a report by HSBC.

44% of companies are also planning to increase their prices, whilst 42% have increased their inventory levels to manage supply disruptions.

Both surging costs and supply disruptions are also causing eight in 10 businesses to rethink their long-term business models, whilst 86% say the trade uncertainty has made them more cautious about expansion and investment.

Meanwhile, 83% of Singapore businesses are less optimistic about compared to their global peers to grow international trade in the coming years.

Considering trade dynamics, one in two Singapore-based businesses plan to increase trade with South Asia, which includes ASEAN, India, and China. Beyond Asia, 46% plan to trade more with Europe, whilst 38% plan to trade more with the Middle East.

data.title: TOTM Technologies CEO, directors resign after EGM bid
data.description:
Their resignation followed requisition notices from nine shareholders.

TOTM Technologies executive director and CEO Irawan Mulyadi has resigned from his position effective 27 May.

Irawan’s resignation will facilitate board and management renewal, following the requisition notices received on 7 May.

Irawan is deemed to be interested in 26,214,297 ordinary shares, representing a 1.92% stake in the company. He also holds 5,000,000 share options granted under the Company's Employee Share Option Scheme 2021.

The company received requisition notices from nine shareholders namely Khoo Thomas Clive, Wong Hong Eng, Tan Ah Ee, Gan Huai Shen, Koh Ah Luan, Vincent Gan, Gan Huai Shi, Ang Siew Joo, and Teo Teo Lee.

The nine requisitioning members, who collectively hold a 10.0% stake in the company, called for an EGM to propose the removal of Irawan and independent directors Dhanie Tri Indrasto and Phang Yew Kiat.

On 28 May, three members withdrew their requisition notices, which resulted in the number of shares held by the requisition members to fall below 10.0%. As a result, TOTM Technologies will take no further action regarding the requisition notices.

The remaining members are seeking to appoint new members within two months to exercise their right to requisition.

In the meantime, the management team will be led by TOTM Technologies’ executive director Pierre Prunier until the board finds a suitable replacement.

data.title: SunMoon Food Company posts $1.5m net loss in FY2025
data.description:
Loss per share stood at $0.168.

SunMoon Food Company reported a net loss of $1.5m in FY2025, reversing the net profit of $28,000 in the previous year.

Loss per share stood at $0.168.

Meanwhile, gross profit remained stable at $821m from $820m a year ago.

Revenue for the period rose 25% year-on-year (YoY) to $33.8m from $27.0m in FY2024. This increase was due to the increased sales of fruits, aquatic foods, and nonhazardous chemical products.

data.title: OxPay to issue 35.4M shares for over $423,000
data.description:
After the subscription, OxPay expects its FY2024 net loss to rise to $3.1m.

OxPay Financial is proposing to issue 35.4 million new shares to existing shareholder Tee Wee Sien (or Zheng Weixian) at an issue price of $0.01197 per share, amounting to a total investment of roughly $423,857.88.

According to OxPay, Tee has indicated his intention to increase his stake in the company and has confirmed that he is not acting on behalf of anyone else in this transaction.

Once the subscription is completed, and assuming that no other new shares are issued on or before completion, Tee will hold an aggregate of 46.6 million shares, giving him approximately a 14.97% stake in the company.

After the share subscription, OxPay expects its FY2024 net loss attributable to the shareholders of the company will increase to $3.1m. However, loss per share will be reduced to $1.

Net tangible assets (NTA) are projected to increase to $1.49m as of FY2024, whilst NTA per share will increase to $0.48.
